locked_facts: Flux depends ONLY on enclosed charge, not on shape of surface
Gaussian surface is a mathematical tool, not physical
Electric flux = E·A·cos(θ) for uniform field
truth_statements: The total electric flux through any closed surface equals the enclosed charge divided by ε₀
The flux through a closed surface does not depend on the shape or size of the surface — only on the enclosed charge
A Gaussian surface is an imaginary mathematical surface chosen for calculation convenience
If a charge is outside the Gaussian surface, its net contribution to the flux through the surface is zero
Gauss's law is most useful when the charge distribution has high symmetry (spherical, cylindrical, or planar)

aha_moment.what_to_show: Show the same charge with two different Gaussian surfaces: a small sphere and a large, irregular blob. Count the field lines passing through each — the same number pierces both surfaces! Φ = Q/ε₀ regardless of surface shape.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: The number of field lines through any closed surface enclosing the charge is the same. Shape and size do not matter — only enclosed charge.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see that Gauss's law is about topology (inside vs outside), not geometry

simulation_states.label: Point charge with radial field lines
simulation_states.description: Positive charge at center with radial field lines. No Gaussian surface yet. Standard point charge field pattern.
simulation_states.emphasis: A point charge +Q with electric field lines radiating outward in all directions

simulation_states.label: Spherical Gaussian surface
simulation_states.description: A wireframe sphere appears around the charge. Every field line passes through it. Caption: Φ = Q/ε₀. The surface is clearly imaginary (wireframe).
simulation_states.emphasis: This wireframe sphere is a Gaussian surface — it is imaginary, chosen for symmetry. Count the field lines passing through it.

simulation_states.label: Different surfaces, same flux!
simulation_states.description: A second, larger and non-spherical Gaussian surface appears alongside the first. The SAME number of field lines pass through both. This is the key insight.
simulation_states.emphasis: SAME charge enclosed → SAME flux, regardless of surface shape or size. This is the power of Gauss's law!

simulation_states.label: Explore — rotate and verify
simulation_states.description: Interactive mode. Both Gaussian surfaces visible. Student rotates to verify that the same lines pass through each surface.
simulation_states.emphasis: Rotate: every line that exits the inner surface also exits the outer one. Gauss's law depends only on enclosed charge.

trigger_keywords: surface ka shape matter karta hai
common_misconceptions.student_belief: The electric flux through a Gaussian surface depends on its shape and size
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: Gauss's law states that the total flux depends ONLY on the enclosed charge: Φ = Q/ε₀. A tiny sphere and a huge irregular surface enclosing the same charge have exactly the same total flux.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show two different Gaussian surfaces around the same charge — same flux through both.

common_misconceptions.student_belief: A Gaussian surface is a real physical object
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: A Gaussian surface is a purely mathematical, imaginary surface that we choose for calculation convenience. It has no physical existence. We choose shapes (sphere, cylinder, plane) that match the symmetry of the charge distribution.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show the wireframe nature of the Gaussian surface — it is clearly not a physical object, just a mathematical tool.

typical_question_types: E due to sphere of charge using Gauss's law
E due to infinite line charge
flux through open surface with charge outside
common_traps: including charges outside the surface in flux calculation
